revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Daigakkō (1869)]][[ja:大学校 (1869年)]]
{{Citation needed|date=April 2023|qq=}} '''Imperial University''' (大学校, Daigakkō) was a government-run educational institution group or administrative agency established in Tokyo in July of the 2nd year of Meiji (August 1869) by the Meiji government. This article also addresses its later renamed form, "'''Imperial University''' (大学, Daigaku)," and its successor institutions, "'''Imperial Southern School''' (大学南校, Daigaku Nankō)" and "'''Imperial Eastern School''' (大学東校, Daigaku Tōkō)." [[File:Shungaku Matsudaira.jpg|200px|right|thumb|Matsudaira Shungaku, appointed the "Imperial Rector" (大学別当, Daigaku Bettō) and head of the university.]] == Overview == The '''Imperial University''' was conceived as a state educational institution integrating the former {{ill|Shoheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoheizaka Academy|qq=}} (renamed as {{ill|Shohei School|ja|昌平学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shohei School|qq=}}), {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}}. It was also intended to serve as a '''central administrative body overseeing school education nationwide'''. Later renamed "Imperial University," it faced internal conflicts between Western studies and traditional Japanese and Chinese studies. The central "Main School" (本校, Honkō) ceased operations early on, and the "Imperial University" as an administrative body was replaced by the {{ill|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|en|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|qid=Q11080185|lt=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|1=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}}, leaving the institution as a single entity largely non-functional. However, this transition solidified the dominance of Western scholars over national and Chinese scholars, leading to the construction of a modern public education system with {{ill|University of Tokyo|en|University of Tokyo|qid=Q7842|lt=The University of Tokyo|1=University of Tokyo}} as its pinnacle. The "Imperial Southern School" formed the foundation of its faculties of {{ill|Jurisprudence|en|Jurisprudence|qid=Q4932206|lt=law|1=Jurisprudence}}, {{ill|science|ja|理学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/science|1=science|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11573530}}, and {{ill|Literature|en|Literature|qid=Q8242|lt=letters|1=Literature}}, while the "Imperial Eastern School" became the predecessor of its {{ill|Medical school|en|Medical school|qid=Q494230|lt=medical faculty|1=Medical school}}.
== History == In April of Keio 4 (May 1868), the new Meiji government seized the educational institutions under the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, including the Confucian {{ill|Shoheizaka Academy|ja|昌平黌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoheizaka Academy|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 昌平坂学問所}}, the Western-focused {{ill|Kaiseisho|ja|開成所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseisho|1=Kaiseijo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11655236}}, and the medical (Western medicine) {{ill|Igakusho|ja|医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Igakusho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Q10950500|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10950500}}. Renamed as {{ill|Shohei School|ja|昌平学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shohei School|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京医学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Medical School|qq=}}, respectively, these institutions reopened from June to September. On July 8 of Meiji 2 (August 15, 1869), the "Imperial University" (大学校, Daigakkō) was established, unifying the three schools. Matsudaira Shungaku was appointed as the "Imperial Rector" (大学別当, Daigaku Bettō), the equivalent of the university president. The university comprised the "Main School" (本校, Honkō) for {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|qid=Q855159|lt=Japanese Studies|1=Kokugaku}} and {{ill|Kangaku|en|Kangaku|qid=Q5646725|lt=Chinese Studies|1=Kangaku}} (reformed from Shohei School) and "Branches" (分局, Bunkyoku) such as Kaisei School, Medical School, and Military School. However, due to disputes and reforms, the "Main School" was closed in 1870. The branches evolved independently, with the [[Daigakkō (1869)#Imperial Southern School|Southern School]] and [[Daigakkō (1869)#Imperial Eastern School|Eastern School]] taking on distinct identities. == Imperial Southern School == Located at the former {{ill|Kaiseisho|ja|開成所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiseisho|1=Kaiseijo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11655236}} in {{ill|Kanda|ja|神田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda|1=Shinden (divine field)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10855080}}, the Southern School focused on {{ill|Western studies|ja|洋学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Western studies|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蘭学|1=Rangaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q877637}}. It initially had courses taught by {{ill|foreign instructors|ja|御雇い外国人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/foreign instructors|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to お雇い外国人|1=Foreign government advisors in Meiji Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q176968}} and offered preparatory and specialized courses. Reorganized and renamed several times, it became the {{ill|Kaisei School|ja|開成学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei School|qq=}}, later integrated into {{ill|University of Tokyo|en|University of Tokyo|qid=Q7842|lt=The University of Tokyo|1=University of Tokyo}}. == Imperial Eastern School == Situated at the former {{ill|Igakusho|ja|医学所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Igakusho|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Q10950500|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10950500}} in {{ill|Shitaya|en|Shitaya|qid=Q11361329|lt=Shitaya|1=Shitaya}}, the Eastern School specialized in Western {{ill|Medicine|en|Medicine|qid=Q11190|lt=medicine|1=Medicine}}. Initially focused on British medicine, it later transitioned to {{ill|German medicine|ja|ドイツ医学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/German medicine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a tradition carried on by {{ill|Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine, University of Tokyo|lt=The University of Tokyo Medical School|ja|東京大学大学院医学系研究科|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine, University of Tokyo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京大学大学院医学系研究科・医学部|1=Graduate School of Medicine and Faculty of Medicine, University of Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11524669}}. == Related Literature == ; Books * Terasaki Masa'o, *The History of The University of Tokyo: A Pioneer in University Systems*, Kodansha, 2007. ISBN 9784061597990 * Amano Ikuo, *The Birth of Universities: The Era of Imperial Universities (Vol. 1)*, Chuko Shinsho, 2009. ISBN 9784121020048 ; Articles * Aso Makoto, *Higher Education Before the Modern School System*, Heibonsha, 1971. * Miyazaki Fumiko, *Imperial Eastern School*, Yushodo Publishing, 1984.
== See Also == * {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|qid=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō|1=Daigaku-ryō}} * {{ill|Gakushūin|ja|学習院 (幕末維新期)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakushūin|1=Gakushūin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11448875}} * {{ill|Shoheizaka Academy|ja|昌平坂学問所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoheizaka Academy|qq=}} {{Tokyo University's Sources and Predecessors|qq=}} {{school-stub|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|大学校_(1869年)}} [[ja:大学校 (1869年)]]
